content.full_text:
#1
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
November 10,
1992
James Scanlon
Deputy County Counsel
Office of the County Counsel
County of Sutter
1160 Civic Center Blvd.
Yuba City,
CA
95993
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-92-549
Dear Mr.
Scanlon:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Sutter County Supervisor Ronald Southard concerning his
duties under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
QUESTION
May Supervisor Southard participate in a decision concerning
a development which is located within 910 feet of property in
which Supervisor Southard has an ownership interest?
CONCLUSION
Supervisor Southard may not participate in the decision j
the development of the property will foreseeably increase or
decrease the fair market value of his real property by $10,000 or
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000,
.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A—92-549
Page 2
more,
or the rental value of the property by at least $1,000 in a
12-month period.
FACTS
The Sutter County Board of Supervisor’s is currently
considering the development of property located in the
jurisdiction.
The developer has requested an exemption from a
county requirement that prior to the approval of a tentative map,
the map must include immediate connection to public sewer and
water service.
The developer has applied to subdivide the property in
question and is asking to be permitted to do so without hooking
the parcels up to the public sewer.
Supervisor Southard owns real
property within 910 feet of the property that is the subject of
the county supervisor’s decisions.
ANALYSIS
Economic Interests
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,
participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest.
Section 87103 specifies that
an official has a financial interest within the meaning of Section
87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have
a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on
the public generally,
on the official or on a member of his or her
immediate family,
or on:
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1000)
or more.
Section 87103(b).
You stated that Supervisor Southard’s residence is 910 feet
away from the property that is the subject of the development
decision.
We assume that Supervisor Southard has an interest in
his home of $1,000 or more.
Consequently,
Supervisor Southard is
prohibited from making, participating in making, or influencing
decisions which would have a reasonably foreseeable and ma€ériál
financial effect on his real property.
Interlinked Decisions
Generally, each governmental decision is analyzed
independently to determine if there will be a foreseeable material
financial effect on an official’s economic interest.
(In re Owen
(1976)
2 FPPC Ops.
77.)
Thus,
we have advised that large,
complex


Our File No.
A—92--549
Page
3
decisions under certain circumstances may be divided into separate
decisions so that an official with a disqualifying interest in one
component of the series of decisions may still participate as to
the other components in which he has no financial interest.
(Merkuloff Advice Letter,
No.
1-90-542; Huffaker Advice Letter,
No.
A—86—343.)
According to your facts, the decisions in question pertain to
the entire prolect and are necessary prerequisites to the project
going forward.
Since a decision concerning the exception from the
requirements of the county ordinance for the development could
affect the project as a whole, the supervisor may not participate
in this interlinked decision unless the project as a whole will
not have a material financial effect on his real property.
2 (
e.g., Miller Advice Letter,
No.
A—82—119;
Nord Advice Letter,
No.
A—82—038.)
Foreseeability and Materiality
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.
An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)
Decisions concerning the development of property in the proximity
of a public official’s real property will clearly have some
foreseeable financial effect on the official’s property.
However, the foreseeable financial effect of a decision must
be material to require disqualification.
If an official’s real
property is directly involved in a decision, generally, the effect
of the decision is deemed to be material and disqualification is
required.
Regulation 18702.1(a) (3) provides a list of situations
in which the supervisor’s real property would be considered
directly involved in a decision.
Your question does not concern a decision that will directly
affect the supervisor’s real property; rather, the decision in
question concerns property that is 910 feet away from his property
which may indirectly affect the supervisor’s real property.
Regulation 18702.3 provides that the effect of a d&1ionön
real property in which an official has an economic interest is
material if:
2
However, later decisions may be separable.
(Athan Advice
Letter,
No. A-86-094.)
You should contact us for further advice
concerning these specific decisions as they arise.


Our File No.
A—92--549
Page 4
(3)
The real property in which the official
has an interest is located outside a radius of 300
feet and any part of the real property is located
within a radius of 2,500 feet of the boundaries
(or
the proposed boundaries)
of the property which is
the subject of the decision and the decision will
have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect of:
(A)
Ten thousand dollars
($10,000)
or
more on the fair market value of the real
property in which the official has an
interest;
or
(B)
Will affect the rental value of the
property by $1,000 or more per 12 month
period.
Since the property that is the subject of the decision is
within 910 feet of Supervisor Southard’s real property, the
supervisor may not participate in the development decisions if
they will foreseeably increase or decrease the fair market value
of real property by $10,000 or more or the rental value of his or
her property by at least $1,000 in a 12-month period.
(Regulation
18702.3(a) (3) (A).)
Regulation 18702.3(d) sets forth factors that must be
considered in determining whether a decision will have a material
financial effect on the supervisor’s real property.
3
These
factors are as follows:
1.
The proximity of the property which is the
subject of the decision and the magnitude of the
proposed project or change in use in relationship
to the property in which the official has an
interest;
2.
Whether it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will affect the development potential
or income producing potential of the property;
3.
[W)hether it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will result in a change to the
character of the neighborhood including, but not
limited to, the effect on traffic, view, privacy,
intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions, or
similar traits of the neighborhood.
The factors to be considered are not limited to the factors
specified in Regulation 18702.3(d).


Our File No.
A—92-549
Page 5
We cannot determine whether the project will have such an
effect on the real property,
we must leave this factual
determination to you and the supervisor.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—59O1.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
Lcj—_—
By:
John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division
SH:JWW:aa
L
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively,
copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 1Oc per page.

,
.

Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
 •

Our File No.
A—92-549

or the rental value of the property by at least $1,000 in a
12-month period.
